---
title: "Ch 5 — Introduction to Euclid's Geometry"
subtitle: "Five postulates, a few axioms, and 2300 years of consequences"
lesson-id: "cbse9.euclids-geometry"
tier: "cbse9"
ncert-class: 9
ncert-chapter: 5
ncert-chapter-title: "Introduction to Euclid's Geometry"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["t1.shapes-angles"]
unlocks: ["cbse9.lines-and-angles", "cbse9.triangles", "cbse9.quadrilaterals", "cbse9.circles"]
applied-domains: ["research"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Do you know what a *line*, a *line segment*, and a *ray* look like?
   *(If no, revisit
   [t1.shapes-angles](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd).)*
2. Can you measure an angle with a protractor?
3. Have you ever drawn a triangle and added up its angles?

Three yes? Read on.
:::

## Touch

Take a sheet of paper and a ruler. Mark two dots, $A$ and $B$. Now lay
your ruler against them and draw a straight line through both.

Try a different angle of the ruler. You can't keep both dots on the line
unless your ruler crosses *exactly* through the same path as before. Two
dots fix one straight line — no more, no fewer.

```{ojs}
viewof ax = Inputs.range([20, 280], {value: 60, step: 5, label: "A.x"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof ay = Inputs.range([20, 220], {value: 180, step: 5, label: "A.y"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof bx = Inputs.range([20, 280], {value: 240, step: 5, label: "B.x"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof by_ = Inputs.range([20, 220], {value: 60, step: 5, label: "B.y"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  // extend the segment to a long straight line
  const dx = bx - ax, dy = by_ - ay;
  const len = Math.hypot(dx, dy) + 1e-9;
  const ex = dx / len, ey = dy / len;
  const x1 = ax - ex * 400, y1 = ay - ey * 400;
  const x2 = bx + ex * 400, y2 = by_ + ey * 400;
  return html`<svg width="320" height="240" style="border:1px solid #ccc">
    <line x1="${x1}" y1="${y1}" x2="${x2}" y2="${y2}" stroke="#888" stroke-dasharray="3,3"/>
    <circle cx="${ax}" cy="${ay}" r="5" fill="#c33"/>
    <circle cx="${bx}" cy="${by_}" r="5" fill="#33c"/>
    <text x="${ax + 8}" y="${ay - 8}" font-size="13">A</text>
    <text x="${bx + 8}" y="${by_ - 8}" font-size="13">B</text>
  </svg>`;
}
```

Move $A$ or $B$. The dashed straight line through both moves with them.
There is **only one** such line, no matter where the points sit.

## Wonder

1. Why do two dots fix exactly one straight line? Could they fix more
   than one? Could they fix none?
2. If you drop a third dot $C$ on the same line, do all three dots also
   fix that line — or could they tell us something new?
3. A geometry book starts with words like "point", "line", "plane". Can
   you actually *define* what a point is, without using another geometry
   word?
4. The first proof in *Elements* shows you can construct an equilateral
   triangle with only a straight edge and a compass. Why might
   mathematicians have begun there?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## A whisper, no answer yet

Q3 is the deepest. Some words have to stay **undefined** — accepted from
direct sensory experience — or every definition would chase its tail
forever. Q4 hints at why we lay down **postulates** before any theorem.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1

Mark two distinct points on a sheet. With a ruler, draw the unique line
through them. With a different ruler placement, try again. What is the
result?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two distinct points determine — how many — straight lines?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Exactly one.** No matter how you orient your ruler, if both points lie
on it, the line drawn is the same. **Postulate (paraphrasing Euclid):
Given two distinct points, there is a unique line passing through them.**
</details>

### Problem 2

Draw a line and pick a point $P$ off the line. With your ruler and
square, try to draw a line through $P$ parallel to the first. How many
such parallels exist?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A line "parallel to" another never meets it. Tilt your second ruler
slightly. Does it still feel parallel?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Exactly one** parallel through $P$. This is **Playfair's axiom**, an
equivalent way to state Euclid's fifth (parallel) postulate.
</details>

### Problem 3

Two segments are each $7$ cm long. A third segment is also $7$ cm long.
Are the first two equal to each other?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"Things equal to the same thing are…"
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Yes, both equal $7$ cm so they equal each other. **Axiom (Common
Notion 1): Things equal to the same thing are equal to one another.**
This is one of Euclid's "common notions" — true of any equal quantities,
not just geometric ones.
</details>

### Problem 4

A segment $AB$ has length $10$ cm. A segment $CD$ has length $4$ cm. We
add a $4$-cm segment to $AB$ and a $4$-cm segment to $CD$. What can we
say about the new total lengths?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$10 \ne 4$. Add the same thing to both. Are the results equal?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AB + 4 = 14$, $CD + 4 = 8$. Still unequal — and unequal by the same
amount, $10 - 4 = 14 - 8 = 6$. **(Common Notion 2):** *If equals are added
to equals, the wholes are equal.* (We did the **non-equal** version: if
the same quantity is added to two unequal quantities, the difference is
preserved.)
</details>

### Problem 5

Construct an equilateral triangle on segment $AB$ using only a straight
edge and a compass. (This is Euclid's Proposition I.1.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Open your compass to length $AB$. Draw two arcs.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
With centre $A$, radius $AB$, draw an arc above the segment. With centre
$B$, radius $AB$, draw a second arc. Where they meet is the third vertex
$C$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. Draw circle with centre $A$, radius $AB$. | Postulate 3 (any segment can be a radius). |
| 2. Draw circle with centre $B$, radius $BA$. | Postulate 3. |
| 3. The two circles meet at a point $C$ above $AB$. | (Euclid took this for granted; modern geometry adds an axiom for it.) |
| 4. Join $AC$ and $BC$. | Postulate 1 (two points determine a line). |
| 5. $AC = AB$ (radii of circle 1). | Definition of circle. |
| 6. $BC = AB$ (radii of circle 2). | Definition of circle. |
| 7. $AC = BC$. | Common Notion 1: things equal to the same thing are equal. |
| 8. Hence $\triangle ABC$ has $AB = BC = CA$ — equilateral. | $\blacksquare$ |

This is the very first theorem in *Elements*. Notice it uses *one*
postulate (radii), one common notion, and produces a fully justified
construction. That is the rhythm of Euclidean proof.
</details>

You discovered: a few self-evident statements (postulates and common
notions) anchor everything. From them, by chained reasoning, every other
geometric fact follows.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **postulate** (Euclid's word) is a geometrical statement accepted
without proof. Euclid's five postulates:

1. A straight line can be drawn from any one point to any other point.
2. A finite straight line can be extended indefinitely.
3. A circle can be drawn with any centre and any radius.
4. All right angles are equal to one another.
5. (Parallel postulate, Playfair's form) Through a point not on a given
   line, exactly one line parallel to the given line can be drawn.

A **common notion** (or axiom) is a general logical principle. The most
used:

- Things equal to the same thing are equal to one another.
- If equals are added to equals, the wholes are equal.
- If equals are subtracted from equals, the remainders are equal.
- The whole is greater than the part.
- Things that coincide with one another are equal.

A **theorem** is a statement *proved* from postulates, common notions,
and previously proved theorems.

A **proof** is a finite chain of statements, each justified, ending in
the desired theorem. A clean proof is a two-column "Statement | Reason"
table.

:::

A short verse:

> *Five postulates Euclid laid as bricks;*
> *Common notions stitched them with logic.*
> *Every theorem since — every dome, every map —*
> *Stands on those few un-proven facts.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Prove: If $A$, $B$, $C$ are three points on a line such that $B$ lies
> between $A$ and $C$, and if a point $M$ is the midpoint of segment
> $AC$, and another point $N$ is the midpoint of segment $AB$, prove
> that $AM > AN$.

A competent solver thinks:

1. $M$ is the midpoint of $AC$, so $AM = \tfrac{1}{2} AC$.
2. $N$ is the midpoint of $AB$, so $AN = \tfrac{1}{2} AB$.
3. Since $B$ lies between $A$ and $C$, $AB$ is *part of* $AC$, so $AC > AB$
   (Common notion: the whole is greater than the part).
4. Halving both sides preserves the inequality:
   $\tfrac{1}{2} AC > \tfrac{1}{2} AB$, i.e. $AM > AN$. $\blacksquare$

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Which of Euclid's postulates says you can extend any line
segment as far as you wish?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Postulate 2.**
</details>

**W2.** Two angles are right angles. What can you say about them?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Postulate 4.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
They are equal — by Postulate 4, *all right angles are equal to one
another*.
</details>

**W3.** If $AB = CD$ and $CD = EF$, what can you conclude about $AB$
and $EF$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AB = EF$ — by Common Notion 1 (things equal to the same thing are equal
to one another).
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Prove that two distinct lines cannot have more than one point in
common.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Suppose for contradiction they did share two points $P$ and $Q$. What
does Postulate 1 say?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. Suppose two distinct lines $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ share two points $P$ and $Q$. | Assumption (for contradiction). |
| 2. Through $P$ and $Q$ there is a unique line. | Postulate 1. |
| 3. So $\ell_1 = \ell_2$. | From 2. |
| 4. But this contradicts "$\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ are distinct". | — |
| 5. Therefore two distinct lines share at most one point. | $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

**P2.** $C$ is the midpoint of segment $AB$, and $D$ is the midpoint of
$AC$. If $AB = 12$ cm, find $AD$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Cut $AB$ in half, then cut that half again.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AC = \tfrac{1}{2}(12) = 6$. $AD = \tfrac{1}{2}(AC) = 3$. **3 cm.**
</details>

**P3.** $AB = AC$ and $AC = AD$. Show that $AB = AD$ and identify the
common notion you used.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AB = AC$ and $AC = AD$. So both $AB$ and $AD$ equal $AC$. By Common
Notion 1, $AB = AD$. $\blacksquare$
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** $X$ and $Y$ are two distinct points, and $\ell$ is the unique
line through them. Suppose another line $m$ also passes through both $X$
and $Y$. Prove that $m = \ell$. Quote the postulate you use.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The phrase "the unique line" is doing a lot of work here.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. By Postulate 1, through $X$ and $Y$ there exists exactly one line. | Postulate 1. |
| 2. $\ell$ passes through $X$ and $Y$. | Given. |
| 3. $m$ passes through $X$ and $Y$. | Given. |
| 4. By the uniqueness from step 1, $m$ and $\ell$ are the same line. | $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

**S2.** A point $P$ is at distance $3$ from line $\ell$. State (without
proof) how many lines through $P$ are parallel to $\ell$, and which
postulate you used.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Exactly one**, by Postulate 5 (in Playfair's form). The fifth
postulate is the most subtle of the five, and dropping it leads to
**non-Euclidean geometries** — the geometry of curved space, used by
Einstein in general relativity.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** "Things which are equal to the same thing are
equal to one another" is:
(a) a postulate (b) a theorem (c) a common notion (d) a definition.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(c) a common notion** (also called an axiom). It is a general logical
principle, not a geometric postulate.
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** State Euclid's first postulate. Use it to
explain why two points determine a unique straight line.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Postulate 1:** A straight line can be drawn from any one point to any
other point. The construction shows existence; the *uniqueness* is
implied by the postulate's modern phrasing — exactly one straight line
passes through two distinct points.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** If $A$, $B$, $C$ are three points on a
line and $AB = BC$, prove that $B$ is the midpoint of $AC$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The midpoint divides a segment into two equal parts. State this
explicitly.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. $A$, $B$, $C$ are collinear with $B$ between $A$ and $C$. | Given. |
| 2. $AB = BC$. | Given. |
| 3. So $B$ divides $AC$ into two equal parts. | From 2 and the meaning of "between". |
| 4. By definition, $B$ is the midpoint of $AC$. | $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** Prove Proposition I.1 of Euclid's *Elements*:
Given a segment $AB$, construct an equilateral triangle on it. Write the
proof as a two-column table.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two arcs from $A$ and $B$ with radius $AB$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
See Discover Problem 5 above. Reproduce the seven-row table verbatim,
citing Postulate 3 (radii), Postulate 1 (joining points), Common Notion 1
(transitivity of equality), and the definition of a circle.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 5, Problem 5.1
(identifying postulates vs axioms, similar to 1-mark). **Previous-year
pointer:** CBSE Class 9 sample paper "state Euclid's postulates and
prove a midpoint claim" (similar to 3-mark and 5-mark).
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Postulate and axiom mean the same thing."** Modern usage tends to
   merge them, but Euclid distinguished: *postulates* are geometric
   (about points, lines, circles), *common notions* (axioms) are
   general logical principles.
2. **"You can prove the parallel postulate."** Two thousand years of
   trying showed you cannot — it is independent of the others. Dropping
   or replacing it gives **non-Euclidean** geometries, which describe
   the actual large-scale structure of the universe.
3. **"A definition of 'point' is just 'a dot'."** Not quite — a point
   has no size, only a position. Even Euclid said "a point is that which
   has no part". Some basic terms are accepted from intuition; that is
   what an *undefined term* means.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you state Euclid's five postulates from memory and explain the
   role of common notions?
2. Did you write each line of the I.1 construction with a justification
   beside it?
3. Could you spot when a step in a proof needs a postulate citation
   rather than a "by inspection"?

- **3 yes:** move on to **Lines and Angles**.
- **2 yes:** redo S1 and S2 — write every reason.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* Problem 5 and *Apply* worked example.
  Proof is a habit; it builds slowly.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`t1.shapes-angles`](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)
  — naming lines, segments, rays, angles.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse9.lines-and-angles`](../06-lines-and-angles/index.qmd) — the
  first major theorem-bundle to prove from these postulates.
- [`cbse9.triangles`](../07-triangles/index.qmd),
  [`cbse9.quadrilaterals`](../08-quadrilaterals/index.qmd), and
  [`cbse9.circles`](../09-circles/index.qmd) — every proof in those
  chapters is grounded here.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 9, Chapter 5 — Introduction to Euclid's Geometry** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 5.1–5.2: definitions,
  postulates, axioms.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** — there is no direct synthesis lesson for
  Euclid's postulates as such; the synthesis curriculum treats geometry
  via [`t1.shapes-angles`](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)
  at a more concrete level. Class 9 is the first year axiomatic geometry
  appears.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The axiomatic method — agree on starting truths, derive
all else by logic — is the spine of every formal subject. Hilbert
re-axiomatised geometry in 1899 to remove gaps Euclid left. Modern proof
assistants (Lean, Coq, Isabelle) verify mathematical proofs the same way
Euclid did, just with computers checking each "Reason" column.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Curved space.** When Einstein replaced Euclid's fifth postulate with a
*curved* notion of "straight line", he produced general relativity. GPS
satellites apply the resulting corrections every second — without them,
your phone's position would drift kilometres per day.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Kiselev (proofs in two-column form, citing
  one postulate or axiom per step), Euclid himself (Proposition I.1
  reproduced as the climax of *Discover*), Hilbert (the modern phrasing
  of "exactly one line through two points").
:::
